news_summary: Today's human rights progress includes successful rescue efforts in Venezuela, financial reforms enhancing UN operations, increased focus on gender diversity in technology, and sustained support for Gaza recovery despite funding challenges.
news_summary_fr: Parmi les avancées enregistrées aujourd'hui en matière de droits de l'homme, on peut citer le succès des opérations de sauvetage menées au Venezuela, les réformes financières visant à renforcer les opérations de l'ONU, l'attention accrue portée à la diversité de genre dans le secteur des technologies, ainsi que le soutien continu apporté à la reconstruction de Gaza malgré les difficultés de financement.
news_summary_es: Los avances de hoy en materia de derechos humanos incluyen operaciones de rescate exitosas en Venezuela, reformas financieras que mejoran las operaciones de la ONU, una mayor atención a la diversidad de género en el ámbito tecnológico y un apoyo constante a la recuperación de Gaza, a pesar de las dificultades de financiación.

articles.title: Three-year-old rescued and taken to hospital six days after Venezuela quake
articles.summary: Video footage shows rescuers cheering as the child is pulled from rubble in La Guaira state.
articles.content:
A three-year-old boy has been pulled alive from the rubble six days after the devastating earthquakes in Venezuela, a Jordanian rescue team has said.

The child, named as Klieber Morán, was pulled from wreckage in La Guaira state, interim President Delcy Rodríguez said. Rodríguez described the child's rescue as a "source of hope for our people".

It comes as UN warned that tens of thousands of people were urgently in need of food and shelter.

The death toll from last week's quakes - with magnitudes of 7.2 and 7.5 - has risen to 1,943 with more than 10,000 people injured and tens of thousands more unaccounted for.

The massive tremors probably damaged or destroyed 58,870 buildings, according to an initial assessment of satellite data from Nasa.

The Jordanian civil defence said Klieber had been given first aid treatment, taken to a hospital and his vital signs were good. He was being treated in the capital Caracas, Venezuelan Assembly President Jorge Rodríguez said.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant and inspiring rescue of a young child alive six days after a devastating earthquake, symbolizing hope and resilience amid a major humanitarian crisis affecting tens of thousands. The rescue has broad emotional and societal impact, highlighting effective emergency response and the potential for survival in disaster situations.
articles.personality_title: Three-year-old rescued alive six days after Venezuela earthquake
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Venezuela was hit by two strong earthquakes last week, with magnitudes of 7.2 and 7.5. The quakes caused widespread damage, killing nearly 2,000 people and injuring over 10,000. Many buildings were destroyed, and thousands of people were left without food or shelter.

**What happened** – A three-year-old boy named Klieber Morán was found alive under the rubble in La Guaira state, six days after the earthquakes. A rescue team from Jordan helped pull him out. He was given first aid and then taken to a hospital in Caracas, where doctors said his vital signs were good.

**Impact** – This rescue is special because it shows that people can survive even after many days trapped in disaster ruins. It gives hope to families and rescue teams working hard in very tough conditions. The event also highlights the importance of quick and skilled emergency responses in saving lives.

**What's next step** – Klieber will continue receiving medical care to recover fully. Rescue teams will keep searching for others who might still be trapped. Meanwhile, aid organizations are working to provide food, shelter, and help to thousands affected by the earthquakes.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A young boy’s survival six days after a powerful earthquake offers a rare and hopeful example amid Venezuela’s disaster recovery efforts.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un garçon de trois ans sauvé vivant six jours après le tremblement de terre au Venezuela
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Venezuela a été frappé la semaine dernière par deux forts tremblements de terre de magnitude 7,2 et 7,5. Ces séismes ont causé de nombreux dégâts, tuant près de 2 000 personnes et en blessant plus de 10 000. De nombreux bâtiments ont été détruits, et des milliers de personnes se sont retrouvées sans nourriture ni abri.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Un garçon de trois ans nommé Klieber Morán a été retrouvé vivant sous les décombres dans l'État de La Guaira, six jours après les tremblements de terre. Une équipe de secours jordanienne l'a aidé à sortir. Il a reçu les premiers soins avant d'être transporté à l'hôpital de Caracas, où les médecins ont confirmé que ses signes vitaux étaient bons.

**Impact** – Ce sauvetage est remarquable car il montre que des personnes peuvent survivre même après plusieurs jours piégées sous les ruines. Cela apporte de l'espoir aux familles et aux équipes de secours qui travaillent dans des conditions difficiles. Cet événement souligne aussi l'importance d'une intervention rapide et compétente pour sauver des vies.

**Prochaine étape** – Klieber continuera de recevoir des soins médicaux pour se rétablir complètement. Les équipes de secours poursuivront leurs recherches pour retrouver d’autres personnes éventuellement coincées. En parallèle, des organisations d’aide fournissent nourriture, abri et assistance aux milliers de personnes touchées.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La survie d’un jeune garçon six jours après un puissant tremblement de terre offre un exemple rare et porteur d’espoir dans les efforts de reconstruction du Venezuela.
articles.personality_title_es: Niño de tres años rescatado con vida seis días después del terremoto en Venezuela
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Venezuela sufrió la semana pasada dos fuertes terremotos de magnitud 7.2 y 7.5. Los sismos causaron graves daños, matando a casi 2,000 personas y dejando más de 10,000 heridas. Muchos edificios fueron destruidos y miles quedaron sin comida ni refugio.

**Qué pasó** – Un niño de tres años llamado Klieber Morán fue encontrado con vida bajo los escombros en el estado de La Guaira, seis días después de los terremotos. Un equipo de rescate jordano ayudó a sacarlo. Recibió primeros auxilios y fue llevado a un hospital en Caracas, donde los médicos dijeron que sus signos vitales estaban bien.

**Impacto** – Este rescate es importante porque muestra que es posible sobrevivir muchos días atrapado después de un desastre. Da esperanza a las familias y a los equipos de rescate que trabajan en condiciones difíciles. También destaca la importancia de respuestas rápidas y bien organizadas para salvar vidas.

**Próximo paso** – Klieber seguirá recibiendo atención médica para recuperarse completamente. Los equipos de rescate continuarán buscando a otras personas que puedan estar atrapadas. Mientras tanto, las organizaciones de ayuda están brindando comida, refugio y apoyo a miles afectados.

**Frase clave** – La supervivencia de un niño pequeño seis días después de un fuerte terremoto ofrece un ejemplo raro y esperanzador en la recuperación de Venezuela.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a small, glowing sapling emerging from cracked, rocky ground symbolizing a child’s survival amid earthquake rubble, with gentle hands of rescuers formed from soft, earthy tones cradling the plant, set against a backdrop of a calm, recovering village silhouette under a soft, hopeful sky.

articles.title: UN scraps rule forcing it to repay money it never received
articles.summary: The United Nations has overhauled a decades-old “Kafkaesque financial rule” that requires it to return unspent funds to Member States - even when those funds had never been deposited in the first place.
articles.content:
The decision taken by the General Assembly on Tuesday follows recommendations from its Fifth Committee, which is responsible for administrative and budgetary matters.

It comes as the UN continues to face a severe liquidity crunch as governments delay paying their mandatory financial contributions, forcing cuts across the system affecting everything from hiring to peacekeeping operations and humanitarian assistance.

Tweet URL

Guterres welcomes key reform

UN Secretary-General António Guterres welcomed the decision in a statement released following the vote, noting that the old financial rules were putting the organization’s “stability at risk.”

“By its vote, the Assembly has agreed to introduce, for a four-year trial period, a new methodology to ensure that unspent funds are returned to Member States only when they are backed by cash,” the UN chief continued.

“This decision will allow us to manage resources, especially for regular and peacekeeping budgets, in a more predictable and responsible way, and to better deliver on the mandates given by Member States.”

‘Critical’ for UN’s immediate future

He described the change as “critical for our immediate operational continuity, especially for peacekeeping operations,” and a major improvement for the next Secretary-General who takes over in January next year.

The UN’s top official will no longer be forced to return funds that were “all too often, never even received in the first place.”

Record level of arrears

The UN ended 2025 with a record high of $1.6 billion in unpaid assessments, according to the Secretary-General latest report on the Organization’s financial status, issued last month.

Overall arrears across the regular budget, peacekeeping and two international tribunals exceed $6.5 billion.

At the beginning of this year, the UN implemented strict cash conservation measures to both reduce and slow expenditures.

“The Secretary-General has requested Member States to either honour their obligations to pay in full and on time or fundamentally overhaul the organization’s financial rules to prevent an imminent collapse,” the report said.

Hamstrung

Until Tuesday night, the financial rules, established 80 years ago, required the UN to give back any unspent funds to Member States as credits against future assessments.

This applied even when underspending results from late contributions or from funds that were never received.

Annalena Baerbock, the General Assembly President, raised the issue in an address to the European Parliament in February.

Highlighting the UN’s “existential liquidity crisis”, she urged European countries “to table proposals to overhaul this Kafkaesque financial rule of reimbursing funds never received.”

Broader message

In her response to the vote, Ms. Baerbock said: “With this landmark resolution, the General Assembly avoided the imminent financial collapse of the UN and modernized an outdated 75-year-old financial rule that has, for too long, undermined the organization's financial stability.”

She said it also sent “a broader message that in fragmented times, Member States of the UN General Assembly can come together to take decisive action.”
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The UN's decision to overhaul an outdated financial rule that forced it to repay money never received addresses a critical liquidity crisis, ensuring operational continuity for peacekeeping and humanitarian missions. This reform has broad significance for global governance and international cooperation, improving the UN's ability to fulfill its mandates effectively.
articles.personality_title: UN updates old financial rule to stop repaying money it never received
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The United Nations has been struggling with a money problem. For many years, it had a rule that forced it to pay back unspent money to countries, even if that money was never actually given to the UN. This caused big problems because some countries delay or don’t pay their fees on time.

**What happened** – On Tuesday, the UN’s General Assembly changed this rule. Now, the UN will only return unspent funds if it actually received the money in cash. This change was recommended by the UN’s Fifth Committee, which handles budget and administrative issues.

**Impact** – This update helps the UN manage its money better, especially for important activities like peacekeeping and humanitarian help. Before, the UN had to give back money it never had, which made planning and operating difficult. This change improves financial stability and makes sure the UN can keep working without sudden money problems.

**What’s next step** – The new rule will be tested for four years to see how well it works. UN leaders hope this will make funding more predictable and help future Secretaries-General run the organization more smoothly. Member countries are also encouraged to pay their fees on time to avoid future money shortages.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN has fixed an outdated financial rule to keep its work steady by only returning money it actually receives from member countries.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’ONU modifie une règle financière ancienne pour ne plus rembourser l’argent jamais reçu
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’Organisation des Nations Unies (ONU) faisait face à un problème d’argent. Pendant des années, une règle l’obligeait à rembourser aux pays les fonds non dépensés, même si cet argent ne lui avait jamais été versé. Cela causait de gros soucis, car certains pays paient leurs cotisations en retard ou pas du tout.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Mardi, l’Assemblée générale de l’ONU a changé cette règle. Désormais, l’ONU ne remboursera des fonds non dépensés que si elle a réellement reçu l’argent en liquide. Ce changement a été recommandé par le Cinquième Comité de l’ONU, qui s’occupe du budget et de l’administration.

**Impact** – Cette mise à jour aide l’ONU à mieux gérer son argent, surtout pour des missions importantes comme le maintien de la paix et l’aide humanitaire. Avant, l’ONU devait rendre de l’argent qu’elle n’avait jamais eu, ce qui rendait la planification difficile. Ce changement améliore la stabilité financière et garantit que l’ONU puisse continuer à fonctionner sans problèmes d’argent soudains.

**Prochaine étape** – La nouvelle règle sera testée pendant quatre ans pour vérifier son efficacité. Les dirigeants de l’ONU espèrent que cela rendra le financement plus prévisible et facilitera la gestion de l’organisation pour les futurs Secrétaires généraux. Les pays membres sont aussi encouragés à payer leurs cotisations à temps pour éviter les pénuries d’argent.

**Phrase-clé** – L’ONU a corrigé une règle financière dépassée pour assurer la continuité de ses missions en ne remboursant que l’argent effectivement reçu des pays membres.
articles.personality_title_es: La ONU actualiza una antigua regla financiera para no devolver dinero nunca recibido
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La Organización de las Naciones Unidas (ONU) tenía un problema de dinero. Durante muchos años, una regla la obligaba a devolver a los países los fondos no gastados, incluso si ese dinero nunca había sido entregado. Esto causaba grandes problemas porque algunos países retrasan o no pagan sus cuotas a tiempo.

**Qué pasó** – El martes, la Asamblea General de la ONU cambió esta regla. Ahora, la ONU solo devolverá fondos no gastados si realmente recibió el dinero en efectivo. Este cambio fue recomendado por el Quinto Comité, que maneja asuntos presupuestarios y administrativos.

**Impacto** – Esta actualización ayuda a la ONU a administrar mejor su dinero, especialmente para actividades importantes como el mantenimiento de la paz y la ayuda humanitaria. Antes, la ONU tenía que devolver dinero que nunca tuvo, lo que dificultaba la planificación y operación. Este cambio mejora la estabilidad financiera y asegura que la ONU pueda seguir trabajando sin problemas de dinero repentinos.

**Próximo paso** – La nueva regla será probada durante cuatro años para ver qué tan bien funciona. Los líderes de la ONU esperan que esto haga que el financiamiento sea más predecible y ayude a los futuros Secretarios Generales a dirigir mejor la organización. También se anima a los países miembros a pagar sus cuotas a tiempo para evitar futuras faltas de dinero.

**Frase clave** – La ONU ha corregido una regla financiera antigua para mantener su trabajo estable devolviendo solo el dinero que realmente recibe de los países miembros.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a large, sturdy balance scale made of natural wood, one side holding a glowing, overflowing treasure chest symbolizing received funds, the other side empty but balanced by transparent, shimmering coins representing unpaid debts; the scale is set against a calm, softly lit backdrop of interlocking puzzle pieces in muted earth tones, symbolizing cooperation and financial reform.

articles.title: Why gender diversity matters in technology
articles.summary: In the face of widespread oppression and commodification of the global south, our hope rests on the strength of our diversity as we move together imperfectly towards our collective liberations.
articles.content:
This editorial was written by Sheena Magenya and Smita V. of the Association for Progressive Communications-Women’s Rights Programme (APC WRP). It is part of Global Voices’ June 2026 Spotlight series, “Gender Diversity.” This series offers insight into gender diversity and how it is being threatened, protected, and preserved around the world. You can support this coverage by donating here.

When NASA was preparing for the launch of Space Shuttle Challenger in 1983, the engineers asked Sally Ride, the first American woman in space, if 100 tampons were enough for a six-day flight. In November 2025, the US Transportation Department unveiled THOR-05F, the first crash test dummy specifically based on a woman’s body, and not just a scaled-down version of the crash test dummy based on men’s bodies. Several new model cars today, by default, unlock all doors as soon as the car is put into “park’ or when the ignition is turned off, which can be particularly unsafe for women and LGBTQ+ people. This setting has to be manually changed. Closer to home, in various social justice spaces and conferences, there are often panels and conversations taking place that only have men speaking and moderating. Colloquially called “manels,” they are a visible indicator and reminder of how the lack of gender diversity in a conversation is not seen as a problem or even as something unusual. The literal absence of women and gender diverse people across all levels of design and decision-making can be, and is often, dangerous and life-threatening to over half the global population.

Inversely, in many contexts where non-heteronormative sexuality and gender expression are legally and socially criminalized, LGBTQ+ people and women are often scapegoats for moral social decay and are positioned as the reason for failed governance or even natural disasters. Through digital misinformation and disinformation, and with widespread platform apathy and inaction, narratives around women and gender-diverse people and communities have been manipulated to promote language that results in violence experienced both online and offline by these communities.

A recent poll by Gallup in the United States, a country previously considered progressive for its inclusion and legislative advancements for women and LGBTIQA+ people, found that, for the first time in nearly two decades, positive public sentiments for same-sex marriage and trans identities (the entire range of diverse trans identities beyond the man–woman binary) and rights are on the downturn. Intolerance for and active exclusion of women and gender diverse people is on the increase, and these shifts do and will continue to have a negative effect on advancements that have helped create a more equitable world.

Gender diversity broadly refers to the equitable representation and inclusion of people of different gender identities and expressions. Improving gender diversity is commonly understood as the intentional inclusion of women in various spaces and fields. It should be understood to also include non-binary, transgender, genderfluid, agender, and other gender-diverse individuals. True gender diversity needs to move beyond traditional binary understandings of gender and look at gender as a galaxy, rather than fixed categories and boxes.

Why does gender diversity matter anyway?

The experiences and lived realities of women and LGBTIQA+ people from all walks of life differ fundamentally from those of people whose lives conform to dominant socially and legally accepted power structures, often presented as normative, such as heterosexuality and patriarchy. This exclusion of women, genders, and sexualities seen as “not normal” to a certain society adversely affects the whole society, as well as the targeted groups and individuals. This exclusion has wide-reaching consequences, including but not limited to the formulation of policies and laws that limit access to life-saving healthcare, foundational education, and identity and travel documents.



Rolling back advancements on gender diversity and inclusion creates unequal societies. Studies have shown that societies that exclude on the basis of gender and sexuality, as well as race, class, caste, disability, and religion, advance much more slowly than societies that create more equal opportunities for the whole community, regardless of differences. The current trend of rejecting and reversing existing initiatives that have sought to right various inequalities (including gender diversity), puts our collective potential for change at risk, and threatens to undo centuries of progress towards a world with better lived experiences for all.

Gender diversity and technology

There is a widespread opinion, or maybe even an aspiration, that technology is perceived as neutral. This is not true or possible because the biases and lack of inclusion from the offline physical worlds get translated into online spaces and are built into technologies, which are then marketed as unbiased. In today’s world, where technology is integral to and enmeshed in our lives and bodies, attention to gender diversity in technology is urgent.

Technologies and their applications need to be supplemented with digital rights and safety conversations that take into specific account how they further widen existing gaps. Facial recognition technology, generative artificial intelligence, and other surveillance technologies are being increasingly used to influence democratic and governance processes and by local authorities in policing, welfare systems and more. The prejudices and biases that exist in the physical world and societies are also reflected in the data used to train these technologies, which replicates the biases against women and gender diverse people, Black people and people of colour, and other structurally marginalised communities.

Large Language Models (LLMs), which power generative AI tools like ChatGPT, Claude, etc., are more likely to give users of African American English lower-prestige jobs, more likely to convict them of a crime, and more likely to sentence them to death rather than life imprisonment for committing murder. A UN Women study found that nearly one in four women human rights defenders surveyed have been subjected to AI-assisted online violence with severe repercussions in the offline world. Without pushing for gender diversity in technology, women and gender diverse persons will continue to be pushed through the gaps in the systems.



Technology-facilitated gender-based violence disproportionately impacts the safety, access, and rights of women, LGBTQ+ persons, and other structurally silenced communities, especially those who are vocal and visible on social media platforms. Because our lives now exist in the continuum of online and offline, this impact is felt in both digital and physical spaces and lives, as well as in the mental health of the survivors.

Dr. Joy Buolawini and Dr. Timnit Gebru’s 2018 landmark study, “Gender Shades,” revealed that facial recognition technology by Microsoft, IBM, and Face++ had the lowest error rates of 0.8 percent with lighter-skinned male faces, and the highest error rates of 34.7 percent when it came to identifying darker female faces. This study and several others that followed led to bans on the use of facial recognition by police departments in several states in the US because of its inbuilt racial bias and capacity to exacerbate structural racial inequalities.

In India, a 50-year-old welfare program denies food rations to pregnant women and new mothers, as the AI facial recognition technology now used to verify identities fails to match the changing faces compared to photos taken years ago as part of identity systems. A recent study showed that popular artificial intelligence models reproduce harmful caste-based stereotypes and bias, and discriminate against people from oppressed castes who are comparatively underrepresented in the media and online. This highlights that, while gender diversity is an essential lens in how we approach digital rights and technologies, this will be incomplete without factoring in the intersectionality of race, caste, location and ability.

The time is now



The current rightward spin of the world and the constant attacks on gender diversity and other social justice interventions by governments, Big Tech, and other powers make it hard for us to push for rights and liberties at this moment. Which is why now is the time to hold the line by continuing to tell our stories and demand presence at the tables where decisions are made in all our intersectional glory. Because we understand that all our oppressions are interconnected, and that the role technology plays in our lives is impossible to escape, we all need to gather our resources, ideas and skills towards more collectivist actions and less siloed approaches to problem solving.



In the face of interconnected systems of heteropatriarchy, racism, settler colonialism, caste oppression, rampant capitalism, militarisation, technoauthoritarianism and commodification of the planet and the global south, our hope rests on the strength of our diversity as we move together imperfectly towards our collective liberations.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the critical importance of gender diversity and inclusion in technology and society, emphasizing the broad societal benefits of equitable representation and the dangers of exclusion. It provides detailed context on how biases in technology impact marginalized groups and calls for collective action to uphold rights and diversity, offering an inspiring and substantive perspective on advancing human rights and social justice.
articles.personality_title: Why gender diversity in technology matters for everyone
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Gender diversity means including people of all gender identities and expressions fairly in society and technology. Right now, many women and gender-diverse people face exclusion and bias in areas like technology, policymaking, and social spaces. This exclusion affects not just them but whole communities.

**What happened** – Experts from the Association for Progressive Communications highlighted examples showing how technology often ignores gender diversity. For instance, the US Transportation Department recently created the first crash test dummy modeled on a woman’s body. Studies also show facial recognition technology struggles to identify darker-skinned women correctly, leading to unfair treatment. Meanwhile, some cars have safety features that put women and LGBTQ+ people at risk unless manually changed. These issues reveal how many technologies are built without considering diverse users.

**Impact** – Ignoring gender diversity in technology and society can cause harm, such as unsafe products, biased AI decisions, and increased violence against marginalized groups. It also slows progress toward fairer societies. When women and gender-diverse people are left out, laws and policies may not protect their health, education, or rights. This exclusion can deepen inequality and hurt everyone’s chances for a better life.

**What's next step** – Experts call for more inclusion of women and gender-diverse people in designing and deciding how technology works. They also urge society to recognize how gender diversity connects with race, class, and other identities. By working together and sharing stories, communities can push for fairer technology and stronger protections against online and offline violence.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Including gender diversity in technology and society is essential to create safer, fairer systems that work well for everyone.
articles.personality_title_fr: Pourquoi la diversité de genre en technologie est importante pour tous
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La diversité de genre signifie inclure équitablement les personnes de toutes identités et expressions de genre dans la société et la technologie. Actuellement, beaucoup de femmes et de personnes de genres divers sont exclues ou victimes de biais dans la technologie, la prise de décisions et les espaces sociaux. Cette exclusion affecte non seulement elles, mais aussi toute la communauté.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des experts de l'Association pour les Communications Progressistes ont montré que la technologie ignore souvent la diversité de genre. Par exemple, le Département américain des Transports a récemment créé le premier mannequin de test de collision basé sur le corps d'une femme. Des études montrent aussi que la reconnaissance faciale a du mal à identifier correctement les femmes à la peau foncée, ce qui entraîne des traitements injustes. De plus, certaines voitures ont des réglages de sécurité qui peuvent être dangereux pour les femmes et les personnes LGBTQ+ s'ils ne sont pas modifiés manuellement. Ces exemples montrent que beaucoup de technologies sont conçues sans penser à tous les utilisateurs.

**Impact** – Ignorer la diversité de genre dans la technologie et la société peut causer des dangers, comme des produits non sûrs, des décisions biaisées de l'IA, et plus de violences contre les groupes marginalisés. Cela ralentit aussi le progrès vers des sociétés plus justes. Quand les femmes et les personnes de genres divers sont exclues, les lois et politiques ne protègent pas leurs droits, leur santé ou leur éducation. Cette exclusion renforce les inégalités et nuit à tous.

**Prochaine étape** – Les experts appellent à plus d'inclusion des femmes et des personnes de genres divers dans la création et la décision sur la technologie. Ils insistent aussi sur l'importance de prendre en compte les liens entre genre, race, classe et autres identités. En travaillant ensemble et en partageant leurs histoires, les communautés peuvent demander des technologies plus justes et une meilleure protection contre la violence en ligne et hors ligne.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Inclure la diversité de genre dans la technologie et la société est essentiel pour créer des systèmes plus sûrs et équitables qui bénéficient à tous.
articles.personality_title_es: Por qué la diversidad de género en la tecnología importa para todos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La diversidad de género significa incluir de manera justa a personas de todas las identidades y expresiones de género en la sociedad y la tecnología. Actualmente, muchas mujeres y personas con diversidad de género enfrentan exclusión y sesgos en tecnología, toma de decisiones y espacios sociales. Esta exclusión afecta no solo a ellas, sino a toda la comunidad.

**Qué pasó** – Expertos de la Asociación para las Comunicaciones Progresistas mostraron que la tecnología a menudo ignora la diversidad de género. Por ejemplo, el Departamento de Transporte de EE.UU. creó recientemente el primer maniquí de prueba de choque basado en el cuerpo de una mujer. Estudios también muestran que la tecnología de reconocimiento facial tiene dificultades para identificar correctamente a mujeres de piel oscura, causando un trato injusto. Además, algunos autos tienen configuraciones de seguridad que pueden ser peligrosas para mujeres y personas LGBTQ+ a menos que se cambien manualmente. Estos ejemplos revelan que muchas tecnologías se diseñan sin considerar a todos los usuarios.

**Impacto** – Ignorar la diversidad de género en tecnología y sociedad puede causar daños, como productos inseguros, decisiones sesgadas de IA y mayor violencia contra grupos marginados. También frena el avance hacia sociedades más justas. Cuando se excluye a mujeres y personas de géneros diversos, las leyes y políticas no protegen sus derechos, salud o educación. Esta exclusión aumenta la desigualdad y afecta a todos.

**Próximo paso** – Los expertos piden más inclusión de mujeres y personas de géneros diversos en el diseño y la toma de decisiones tecnológicas. También destacan la importancia de reconocer cómo la diversidad de género se conecta con la raza, clase y otras identidades. Trabajando juntos y contando sus historias, las comunidades pueden exigir tecnologías más justas y mejor protección contra la violencia en línea y fuera de ella.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Incluir la diversidad de género en la tecnología y la sociedad es esencial para crear sistemas más seguros y justos que funcionen para todos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a diverse constellation of glowing stars and planets shaped like abstract female and non-binary silhouettes, interconnected by threads of light symbolizing technology and inclusion, floating in a calm cosmic space that blends soft natural hues of blue, beige, and gentle gold.

articles.title: Despite record $100 million shortfall, Palestine relief agency still ‘a critical platform’ for Gaza recovery
articles.summary: The UN agency serving 5.9 million Palestine refugees, UNRWA, continues to strive to deliver on its mandate while facing an unprecedented $100 million budget shortfall, a gap it hopes to narrow during Tuesday’s pledging conference at UN Headquarters.
articles.content:
Operating primarily on voluntary donations since its inception in the late 1940s, UNRWA continues to provide lifesaving and essential services in the Occupied Palestinian Territory, from school programmes to polio vaccines, with the UN chief calling it a “lifeline” to Palestinians.

However, UN Secretary-General António Guterres raised sharp concerns on Tuesday about the agency’s cash shortfall, which he said “has grave implications for the entire region”.

“UNRWA is a stabilising force in an age of instability, registering, protecting and assisting highly vulnerable populations and countering the hopelessness that can fuel insecurity,” he said.

Nominal ceasefire

A pendulum of pledges followed the October 2023 Hamas-led attack on Israel that left 1,200 dead and 250 taken hostage, with subsequent Israeli strikes that turned much of Gaza into rubble, displaced 80 per cent of the population and killed at least 70,000 people, according to local authorities.

Tweet URL

Since 2023, attacks on UNRWA have left some 390 staff dead and UN property bombed, striking such critical services as schools and hospitals alongside disinformation drives, with allegations in early 2024 that some agency staff were complicit in attacks on Israel.

While the allegations were independently investigated and nations, except the United States, have thawed their frozen funding alongside growing private donations, a $100 million gap remains.

Unkept pledging promises

Every year, Member States gather to pledge their support for the agency, but what is promised is not always what arrives, making it challenging to operate and has forced the agency to cut back on what it is mandated to deliver.

For example, in 2025, pledges reached $878 million, but UNRWA received only $839 million, a pattern also seen in 2024.

“They cannot keep going like this without urgent backing and financial support from Member States,” Mr. Guterres said, adding that the liquidity crisis also jeopardises UNWRA’s ability to implement its mandate, given by the General Assembly and renewed six months ago with overwhelming Member State support.

UN chief appalled by smear campaigns

Appalled by continued efforts to marginalise or tarnish UNRWA’s critical work with diplomatic roadblocks and smear campaigns, the UN Secretary-General said “UNRWA has taken decisive steps to ensure its house is in order.”

That included already implementing more than 40 recommendations from the 2024 independent Colonna report, he said.

“I’m counting on you to keep speaking up for the agency,” he said, urging them to also pledge support for UNRWA’s essential work.

UN News

‘Critical platform for recovery’

At the same time, the work of UNRWA remains unchanged, even though funding shortfalls have meant reduced service delivery and other cost-saving measures.

Tweet URL

Indeed, needs have grown since the war and amid the recovery, where most of Gaza’s 2.3 million residents remain in shelters and tents facing food insecurity and a dearth of essential services.

Today, with the stuttering ceasefire in Gaza stretching to eight months, nearly 1.7 million people still depend on UNRWA, which operates like a civil service, said Christian Saunders, the agency’s acting Commissioner-General.

“UNRWA is a critical platform for recovery, institutional stability and lasting peace,” he said, underscoring that its capacities are “vital” for:

Implementing Security Council resolution 2803 (2025) that recognised the Hamas-Israel ceasefire in Gaza and the [President Donald] Trump Declaration for Enduring Peace and Prosperity

Efforts of President Trump’s Board of Peace

The international committee for the administration of Gaza

Among UN’s first aid agencies

Established by the General Assembly in 1948, UNRWA was never meant to be permanent, but hinged on the notion of the creation of a Palestinian State alongside Israel.

Nearly eight decades later, the UN agency continues to provide such lifechanging services as access to clean water, education, healthcare and hope, the UN General Assembly President Annalena Baerbock told Member States gathered at the pledging conference.

“A Palestinian State means that UNRWA is no longer needed,” she said. “Until that day comes, let us never stop working for the two-State solution and let us never stop supporting UNRWA.”

What exactly does UNRWA do?

Click the links below to learn more about the UN agency’s critical work in occupied Palestinian territory from 1949 to now:

Watch the UNRWA pledging conference on UN Web TV:
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the ongoing vital humanitarian work of UNRWA providing essential services such as education, healthcare, and food security to millions of Palestinian refugees despite severe funding shortfalls and operational challenges. This work has broad significance for a vulnerable population and represents a critical platform for stability and recovery in a conflict-affected region. The article provides detailed context on the agency's impact, challenges, and international efforts to support it, making it a substantive and meaningful positive story.
articles.personality_title: UN agency UNRWA fights $100 million shortfall to support millions in Gaza
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Since 1949, the United Nations Relief and Works Agency (UNRWA) has helped Palestinian refugees by providing important services like schools, healthcare, and clean water. Today, it supports nearly 6 million people living in difficult conditions, especially in Gaza.

**What happened** – UNRWA is facing a large budget gap of $100 million, making it hard to keep offering these services. This shortfall comes after a deadly conflict in 2023 that caused great damage in Gaza and displaced most of its population. Despite attacks on its staff and facilities, UNRWA continues its work. The agency hopes to reduce this funding gap at a recent pledging conference held at the United Nations headquarters.

**Impact** – UNRWA’s work is crucial because it provides life-saving help to about 1.7 million people in Gaza who still live in shelters and face shortages of food and medical care. The agency also supports peace efforts and stability in a region affected by ongoing conflict. Without enough money, UNRWA must cut back on services, which could make life harder for many vulnerable families.

**What’s next step** – UNRWA and the UN Secretary-General are asking countries to keep their promises to fund the agency fully. They are also working to improve transparency and trust after past allegations. Continued support at upcoming meetings will be important to help UNRWA maintain and possibly expand its aid in the months ahead.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Despite serious money shortages, UNRWA remains a vital lifeline for millions of Palestinian refugees and a key partner in Gaza’s recovery and stability.
articles.personality_title_fr: L'agence de l'ONU UNRWA lutte contre un déficit de 100 millions de dollars pour soutenir des millions de personnes à Gaza
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis 1949, l'Agence des Nations Unies pour l'aide aux réfugiés palestiniens (UNRWA) fournit des services essentiels comme l'éducation, la santé et l'eau propre. Aujourd'hui, elle aide près de 6 millions de personnes vivant dans des conditions difficiles, notamment à Gaza.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – L'UNRWA fait face à un déficit budgétaire de 100 millions de dollars, ce qui complique la poursuite de ses services. Ce manque de fonds survient après un conflit meurtrier en 2023 qui a causé de grands dégâts à Gaza et déplacé la majorité de sa population. Malgré des attaques contre son personnel et ses installations, l'agence continue son travail. Elle espère réduire ce déficit lors d'une récente conférence de dons organisée au siège des Nations Unies.

**Impact** – Le travail de l'UNRWA est crucial car il apporte une aide vitale à environ 1,7 million de personnes à Gaza, encore réfugiées dans des abris et confrontées à des pénuries alimentaires et médicales. L'agence soutient aussi les efforts de paix et la stabilité dans une région marquée par le conflit. Sans financement suffisant, l'UNRWA doit réduire ses services, ce qui pourrait aggraver la situation des familles vulnérables.

**Prochaine étape** – L'UNRWA et le Secrétaire général de l'ONU demandent aux pays de respecter leurs promesses de financement. Ils travaillent aussi à renforcer la transparence et la confiance après des controverses passées. Un soutien continu lors des prochaines réunions sera essentiel pour maintenir et peut-être étendre l'aide de l'agence.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Malgré un grave manque de fonds, l'UNRWA reste un soutien vital pour des millions de réfugiés palestiniens et un acteur clé de la reconstruction et de la stabilité à Gaza.
articles.personality_title_es: La agencia de la ONU UNRWA lucha contra un déficit de 100 millones para apoyar a millones en Gaza
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Desde 1949, la Agencia de Naciones Unidas para los Refugiados Palestinos (UNRWA) ha brindado servicios esenciales como educación, salud y agua limpia. Hoy, apoya a casi 6 millones de personas que viven en condiciones difíciles, especialmente en Gaza.

**Qué pasó** – UNRWA enfrenta un déficit presupuestario de 100 millones de dólares, lo que dificulta la continuidad de sus servicios. Este déficit llega después de un conflicto mortal en 2023 que causó grandes daños en Gaza y desplazó a la mayoría de su población. A pesar de ataques contra su personal e instalaciones, la agencia continúa trabajando. Espera reducir este déficit en una reciente conferencia de donaciones en la sede de la ONU.

**Impacto** – El trabajo de UNRWA es fundamental porque ofrece ayuda vital a unos 1,7 millones de personas en Gaza que aún viven en refugios y enfrentan escasez de alimentos y atención médica. La agencia también apoya los esfuerzos de paz y la estabilidad en una región afectada por el conflicto. Sin suficiente dinero, UNRWA debe recortar servicios, lo que podría dificultar la vida de muchas familias vulnerables.

**Próximo paso** – UNRWA y el Secretario General de la ONU piden a los países que cumplan sus promesas de financiamiento. También están trabajando para mejorar la transparencia y la confianza tras acusaciones anteriores. El apoyo continuo en futuras reuniones será clave para ayudar a UNRWA a mantener y posiblemente ampliar su ayuda.

**Conclusión en una frase** – A pesar de una grave falta de fondos, UNRWA sigue siendo un apoyo vital para millones de refugiados palestinos y un socio clave en la recuperación y estabilidad de Gaza.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a sturdy olive tree with deep roots spreading across a cracked, arid land symbolizing Gaza, its branches sheltering a cluster of simple, hand-drawn schoolbooks, a vaccine vial, and a water jug, all bathed in soft, natural earth tones, conveying resilience, hope, and essential humanitarian support amidst hardship.
